symptoms: Wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and persistent coughing, especially at night or early morning.
causes: Chronic airway inflammation triggered by allergens, air pollution, respiratory infections, exercise, or cold air.
prevention: Avoid known triggers, follow asthma action plans, use inhaled corticosteroids as prescribed, and monitor symptoms regularly.
treatment: Inhaled bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol), corticosteroids, leukotriene modifiers, and biologics for severe cases.

symptoms: Increased thirst, frequent urination, unexplained weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision, and slow wound healing.
causes: Type 1: autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells. Type 2: insulin resistance often due to obesity, inactivity, and genetics.
prevention: Healthy diet, regular exercise, weight management, and routine glucose screening for at-risk individuals.
treatment: Type 1: insulin therapy. Type 2: oral antidiabetics (e.g., metformin), lifestyle changes, and sometimes insulin.

symptoms: Often asymptomatic; may include headaches, dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, or visual disturbances in advanced stages.
causes: Primary: unknown. Secondary: kidney disease, hormonal disorders, certain medications, or sleep apnea.
prevention: Low-sodium diet, regular physical activity, weight loss, stress reduction, and limiting alcohol intake.
treatment: ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, calcium channel blockers, and lifestyle changes.

symptoms: Pulsating headache, often on one side, with nausea, vomiting, light and sound sensitivity; some experience aura.
causes: Genetic and neurological factors; triggered by stress, hormonal changes, certain foods, or sleep disturbances.
prevention: Identifying and avoiding triggers, maintaining consistent sleep and diet, and using prophylactic medications if frequent.
treatment: NSAIDs, triptans, antiemetics, and preventive drugs such as beta-blockers or CGRP inhibitors.

symptoms: Fever, dry cough, fatigue, shortness of breath, sore throat, loss of taste or smell, and body aches.
causes: Infection by SARS-CoV-2 virus, transmitted via respiratory droplets, aerosols, and contaminated surfaces.
prevention: Vaccination, mask-wearing, hand hygiene, physical distancing, and avoiding crowded spaces.
treatment: Supportive care, antivirals (e.g., remdesivir), corticosteroids for severe cases, and oxygen therapy when required.

symptoms: Cough with phlegm, chest pain, fever, chills, and difficulty breathing.
causes: Infection of lung tissue by bacteria (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae), viruses, or fungi.
prevention: Vaccination (e.g., pneumococcal, influenza), smoking cessation, and infection control practices.
treatment: Antibiotics for bacterial pneumonia, antivirals if applicable, supportive care, and hospitalization in severe cases.

symptoms: Fatigue, pale skin, dizziness, shortness of breath, cold extremities, and rapid heartbeat.
causes: Iron deficiency, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, chronic disease, blood loss, or bone marrow disorders.
prevention: Balanced diet with iron and vitamins, regular screening, and managing chronic illnesses.
treatment: Iron supplements, dietary changes, vitamin B12 or folate replacement, and transfusions in severe cases.

symptoms: Joint pain, swelling, stiffness, reduced mobility, and sometimes warmth over affected joints.
causes: Osteoarthritis: wear-and-tear. Rheumatoid arthritis: autoimmune. Other types: infections, uric acid buildup, or injury.
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, joint protection, regular physical activity, and early treatment of joint injuries.
treatment: NSAIDs, corticosteroids, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), and physical therapy.

symptoms: Persistent sadness, loss of interest, fatigue, sleep disturbances, feelings of guilt, and thoughts of suicide.
causes: Biological, psychological, and environmental factors including genetics, trauma, and chronic stress.
prevention: Early intervention, stress management, social support, and regular mental health checkups.
treatment: Psychotherapy (e.g., CBT), antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs), lifestyle changes, and in severe cases, electroconvulsive therapy (ECT).

symptoms: Recurrent seizures, temporary confusion, staring spells, muscle jerking, and loss of consciousness.
causes: Genetic factors, brain injury, stroke, infections, or developmental disorders; often idiopathic.
prevention: Preventing head trauma, prenatal care, and treating infections early; not always preventable.
treatment: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., valproate, levetiracetam), vagus nerve stimulation, and surgery in refractory cases.

symptoms: Progressive memory loss, confusion, difficulty with language, disorientation, and changes in behavior and personality.
causes: Accumulation of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles in the brain; influenced by aging and genetic factors.
prevention: Regular physical activity, cognitive engagement, heart-healthy diet, and management of cardiovascular risk factors.
treatment: Cholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., donepezil), memantine, supportive care, and structured routines.

symptoms: Sudden weakness (especially on one side), difficulty speaking, vision problems, dizziness, and loss of balance or consciousness.
causes: Blocked blood flow to the brain (ischemic stroke) or bleeding in the brain (hemorrhagic stroke).
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and diabetes; avoiding smoking and excess alcohol; regular exercise.
treatment: Thrombolytics for ischemic stroke, surgery for hemorrhagic stroke, rehabilitation, and secondary prevention.

symptoms: Tremors, slowed movement (bradykinesia), stiffness, balance problems, and facial masking.
causes: Loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra; cause often idiopathic or linked to genetic/environmental factors.
prevention: No known prevention; healthy lifestyle and regular exercise may reduce risk or delay onset.
treatment: Levodopa-carbidopa, dopamine agonists, MAO-B inhibitors, physical therapy, and deep brain stimulation.

symptoms: Varies by type; may include unexplained weight loss, fatigue, pain, lumps, abnormal bleeding, or changes in bowel/bladder habits.
causes: Uncontrolled cell division due to genetic mutations; triggered by carcinogens, viruses, or inherited factors.
prevention: Avoid smoking, limit alcohol, healthy diet, exercise, vaccination (e.g., HPV, HBV), and regular screening.
treatment: Surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, immunotherapy, and targeted therapy based on cancer type.

symptoms: Fatigue, frequent infections, easy bruising or bleeding, weight loss, and swollen lymph nodes or spleen.
causes: Uncontrolled growth of abnormal white blood cells; linked to genetic mutations and radiation or chemical exposure.
prevention: No guaranteed prevention; reducing exposure to carcinogens and regular health checkups may help.
treatment: Chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplantation, and targeted biological agents.

symptoms: Painless lymph node swelling, fatigue, night sweats, fever, and unexplained weight loss.
causes: Cancer of the lymphatic system; exact cause unknown but involves genetic and environmental factors, including viral infections (e.g., EBV).
prevention: No clear prevention; maintaining a healthy immune system and minimizing exposure to known risk factors may help.
treatment: Chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy (e.g., rituximab), and stem cell transplantation.

symptoms: Lump in the breast or armpit, changes in breast shape or skin, nipple discharge, and breast pain.
causes: Genetic mutations (e.g., BRCA1/2), hormonal factors, lifestyle, and family history.
prevention: Regular screening (mammograms), self-exams, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, and risk-reducing surgery for high-risk individuals.
treatment: Surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, hormonal therapy (e.g., tamoxifen), and targeted drugs (e.g., HER2 inhibitors).

symptoms: Persistent cough, chest pain, coughing up blood, shortness of breath, and unexplained weight loss.
causes: Most commonly caused by smoking; also due to radon exposure, asbestos, and genetic susceptibility.
prevention: Avoid smoking, limit exposure to occupational toxins, and participate in screening programs if at risk.
treatment: Surgical resection, chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy, and targeted therapy depending on cancer type.

symptoms: Changes in bowel habits, blood in stool, abdominal pain, fatigue, and unintended weight loss.
causes: Genetic mutations (e.g., APC gene), lifestyle factors, inflammatory bowel disease, and family history.
prevention: Regular colonoscopy screening, high-fiber diet, physical activity, limiting red/processed meat, and managing polyps.
treatment: Surgical removal of tumors, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and targeted monoclonal antibodies.

symptoms: New or changing moles, non-healing sores, rough/scaly patches, or pigmented lesions.
causes: DNA damage from ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure, tanning beds, and genetic predisposition.
prevention: Use sunscreen, wear protective clothing, avoid excessive sun exposure, and perform regular skin checks.
treatment: Surgical excision, cryotherapy, topical treatments, radiation, and immunotherapy (especially for melanoma).

symptoms: Persistent cough (sometimes with blood), night sweats, fever, fatigue, weight loss, and chest pain.
causes: Infection by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, spread through airborne droplets from coughing or sneezing.
prevention: BCG vaccination, proper ventilation, early detection, and treatment of active cases.
treatment: Combination antibiotic therapy for at least 6 months, including isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol.

symptoms: Jaundice, fatigue, abdominal discomfort, dark urine, and nausea; symptoms vary by hepatitis type.
causes: Viral infections (HAV, HBV, HCV), alcohol abuse, autoimmune reactions, or drug-induced liver injury.
prevention: Vaccination (for HAV and HBV), safe injection practices, safe sex, and avoiding contaminated food or water.
treatment: Supportive care for HAV, antiviral drugs for HBV/HCV (e.g., entecavir, sofosbuvir), and liver monitoring.

symptoms: Fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, recurrent infections, and opportunistic illnesses like pneumonia or tuberculosis.
causes: Infection by human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), transmitted via blood, sexual contact, or mother-to-child during birth.
prevention: Safe sex practices, PrEP for high-risk individuals, regular testing, and avoiding shared needles.
treatment: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) using drug combinations to suppress viral load and restore immune function.

symptoms: Cyclic fever, chills, headache, nausea, muscle aches, and sweating.
causes: Infection by *Plasmodium* parasites, transmitted through the bite of infected *Anopheles* mosquitoes.
prevention: Insecticide-treated bed nets, antimalarial prophylaxis, mosquito control, and protective clothing.
treatment: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) and supportive care in severe cases.

symptoms: High fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, and mild bleeding (e.g., nose or gums).
causes: Infection by dengue virus, transmitted by *Aedes* mosquitoes (especially *A. aegypti*).
prevention: Eliminating mosquito breeding sites, using repellents, and protective clothing; dengue vaccine available in some regions.
treatment: Supportive care with fluids, acetaminophen for fever; avoid NSAIDs due to bleeding risk.

symptoms: Mild fever, rash, conjunctivitis, joint pain, and headache; often asymptomatic.
causes: Infection by Zika virus, spread primarily by *Aedes* mosquitoes or through sexual transmission.
prevention: Mosquito control, insect repellents, protected sex, and avoiding travel to high-risk areas during outbreaks.
treatment: Supportive care with fluids and rest; no specific antiviral therapy available.

symptoms: Fever, muscle pain, vomiting, diarrhea, bleeding, and organ failure in severe cases.
causes: Ebola virus infection, transmitted via contact with bodily fluids of infected individuals or animals.
prevention: Avoiding exposure in endemic areas, protective gear for healthcare workers, and rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine in outbreak zones.
treatment: Supportive care (hydration, oxygen), monoclonal antibodies, and investigational antivirals in specialized centers.

symptoms: Itchy rash with red spots and blisters, fever, fatigue, and loss of appetite.
causes: Infection by varicella-zoster virus (VZV), spread through airborne droplets or direct contact.
prevention: Varicella vaccination and isolation of infected individuals to prevent spread.
treatment: Supportive care (antihistamines, acetaminophen), and antiviral therapy (acyclovir) in high-risk or severe cases.

symptoms: High fever, cough, runny nose, conjunctivitis, and a characteristic red rash starting on the face.
causes: Infection by measles virus, highly contagious via respiratory droplets.
prevention: MMR vaccination, herd immunity, and isolation during outbreaks.
treatment: Supportive care, vitamin A supplementation, and management of complications like pneumonia.

symptoms: Painful swelling of the salivary glands (especially parotid), fever, headache, muscle aches, and difficulty chewing.
causes: Mumps virus infection, spread through saliva or respiratory secretions.
prevention: MMR vaccination and avoiding contact with infected individuals.
treatment: Supportive care with hydration, analgesics, and cold compresses; most recover without complications.

symptoms: Mild fever, pink rash starting on the face, swollen lymph nodes, joint pain, and conjunctivitis.
causes: Rubella virus infection, transmitted via respiratory droplets; particularly dangerous during pregnancy.
prevention: MMR vaccination and avoiding exposure during pregnancy.
treatment: Supportive care; symptoms usually resolve within a week in healthy individuals.

symptoms: Fever, sore throat, muscle weakness, and in severe cases, paralysis and respiratory difficulty.
causes: Poliovirus infection, transmitted via fecal-oral route or contaminated water.
prevention: Polio vaccination (IPV or OPV) as part of routine immunization schedules.
treatment: Supportive care, physical therapy, and ventilatory support in severe cases.

symptoms: High fever, chills, sore throat, muscle aches, cough, fatigue, and nasal congestion.
causes: Influenza viruses (A, B, C) spread via respiratory droplets or contact with contaminated surfaces.
prevention: Annual flu vaccination, hand hygiene, mask use, and avoiding close contact with sick individuals.
treatment: Antiviral medications (e.g., oseltamivir) if given early, rest, fluids, and symptom relief.

symptoms: Cough with mucus, chest discomfort, wheezing, and shortness of breath.
causes: Acute: viral infections; Chronic: long-term exposure to irritants like smoking or air pollution.
prevention: Avoid smoking and air pollutants; get vaccinated for flu and pneumonia.
treatment: Supportive care for acute cases; bronchodilators, steroids, and pulmonary rehab for chronic bronchitis.

symptoms: Sore throat, painful swallowing, swollen tonsils, fever, and tender lymph nodes.
causes: Viral or bacterial infections, most commonly *Streptococcus pyogenes* (strep throat).
prevention: Good hygiene, avoiding contact with infected individuals, and early treatment of sore throat.
treatment: Rest, fluids, antibiotics if bacterial, and tonsillectomy for recurrent cases.

symptoms: Burning urination, frequent urge to urinate, cloudy or bloody urine, and pelvic discomfort.
causes: Bacterial infection, most commonly *Escherichia coli* entering the urinary tract.
prevention: Hydration, urinating after sex, wiping front to back, and good hygiene.
treatment: Oral antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) and increased fluid intake.

symptoms: Swelling (edema), fatigue, changes in urination, nausea, and confusion in advanced stages.
causes: Diabetes, hypertension, glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease, or drug toxicity.
prevention: Blood pressure and glucose control, avoiding NSAIDs and nephrotoxins, and regular screenings.
treatment: Diet modification, medications, dialysis in end-stage disease, and kidney transplant if indicated.

symptoms: Jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, swelling, dark urine, and easy bruising.
causes: Hepatitis viruses, alcohol abuse, fatty liver, autoimmune disease, or toxins.
prevention: Avoid excessive alcohol, hepatitis vaccination, maintain healthy weight, and monitor medications.
treatment: Treat underlying cause, antiviral or immunosuppressive therapy, and liver transplant in severe cases.

symptoms: Severe abdominal pain radiating to the back, nausea, vomiting, and fever.
causes: Gallstones, chronic alcohol use, high triglycerides, certain medications, or infections.
prevention: Limit alcohol intake, manage cholesterol, and avoid known trigger medications.
treatment: Fasting, IV fluids, pain management, and treating underlying cause (e.g., gallstone removal).

symptoms: Sudden abdominal pain (often starting near the navel and shifting to the lower right), fever, nausea, and loss of appetite.
causes: Blockage of the appendix by feces, inflammation, or infection leading to swelling and rupture risk.
prevention: No specific prevention; early diagnosis and treatment reduce complications.
treatment: Surgical removal of the appendix (appendectomy); antibiotics may be used pre-operatively.

symptoms: Sudden upper right abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and possible jaundice if bile ducts are blocked.
causes: Crystallized cholesterol or bilirubin forming stones in the gallbladder; linked to obesity, diet, or rapid weight loss.
prevention: Healthy diet, regular exercise, maintaining healthy weight, and gradual weight loss.
treatment: Surgical removal of the gallbladder (cholecystectomy) or medications to dissolve stones.

symptoms: Excess body fat, shortness of breath, joint pain, fatigue, and increased risk of chronic diseases.
causes: Long-term energy imbalance due to high caloric intake, sedentary lifestyle, genetic and hormonal factors.
prevention: Balanced diet, regular physical activity, behavioral therapy, and early lifestyle interventions.
treatment: Diet and exercise programs, medications for weight loss, bariatric surgery in severe cases.

symptoms: Extreme weight loss, fear of gaining weight, distorted body image, fatigue, and amenorrhea in females.
causes: Psychological, societal, and genetic factors; often coexists with anxiety or perfectionism.
prevention: Early psychological intervention, body positivity education, and mental health support in adolescents.
treatment: Nutritional rehabilitation, psychotherapy (CBT), family-based therapy, and medical monitoring.

symptoms: Binge eating followed by purging (vomiting, laxatives), weight fluctuations, sore throat, and dental erosion.
causes: Psychological factors including low self-esteem, societal pressure, and trauma history.
prevention: Healthy coping strategies, early detection, and mental health education.
treatment: Psychotherapy (CBT), antidepressants (SSRIs), nutritional counseling, and support groups.

symptoms: Abnormal curvature of the spine, uneven shoulders or waist, and back pain in severe cases.
causes: Idiopathic (most common), congenital spinal defects, or neuromuscular disorders.
prevention: No known prevention for idiopathic type; early screening in children is key.
treatment: Monitoring, bracing in growing children, and spinal surgery in severe deformities.

symptoms: Gradual loss of peripheral vision, tunnel vision in advanced stages, and eye pain in acute cases.
causes: Increased intraocular pressure damaging the optic nerve; can also occur with normal pressure.
prevention: Regular eye exams, especially for high-risk groups; early treatment can preserve vision.
treatment: Eye drops to lower pressure, oral medications, laser therapy, or surgery.

symptoms: Cloudy or blurred vision, glare sensitivity, faded colors, and difficulty seeing at night.
causes: Aging, diabetes, UV exposure, smoking, or trauma leading to lens clouding.
prevention: Wearing sunglasses, managing chronic conditions, and avoiding smoking.
treatment: Surgical removal of the cloudy lens and implantation of an artificial lens.

symptoms: Facial pain or pressure, nasal congestion, thick nasal discharge, headache, and postnasal drip.
causes: Viral, bacterial, or allergic inflammation of the sinuses following upper respiratory infections.
prevention: Avoiding allergens, nasal irrigation, and managing colds promptly.
treatment: Decongestants, nasal corticosteroids, saline rinses, and antibiotics if bacterial.

symptoms: Dry, itchy, red, and inflamed skin; often appears on the hands, elbows, or behind the knees.
causes: Genetic predisposition, immune dysfunction, environmental irritants, and allergens.
prevention: Moisturizing regularly, avoiding triggers, using hypoallergenic products.
treatment: Topical corticosteroids, antihistamines, moisturizers, and immunosuppressive creams in severe cases.

symptoms: Thick, scaly, red patches of skin commonly on elbows, knees, and scalp; may be itchy or painful.
causes: Autoimmune skin condition with genetic and environmental triggers like stress or infections.
prevention: Stress management, avoiding skin injuries and infections, and lifestyle adjustments.
treatment: Topical treatments (steroids, vitamin D analogs), phototherapy, systemic drugs, and biologics.

symptoms: Pimples, blackheads, whiteheads, and cysts typically on the face, chest, and back.
causes: Clogged hair follicles due to excess oil, dead skin cells, bacteria, and hormonal changes.
prevention: Gentle skin care, avoiding oily products, managing stress, and not picking at pimples.
treatment: Topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, antibiotics, hormonal therapy, and isotretinoin for severe cases.

symptoms: Loss of skin color in patches due to pigment cell destruction; often affects face, hands, and joints.
causes: Autoimmune destruction of melanocytes; may be linked to genetic or environmental factors.
prevention: No definitive prevention; sun protection and managing autoimmune triggers may help.
treatment: Topical corticosteroids, phototherapy, depigmentation therapy, or skin camouflage cosmetics.

symptoms: Burning stomach pain, bloating, nausea, and indigestion; symptoms worsen on an empty stomach.
causes: Infection with *Helicobacter pylori* or prolonged NSAID use leading to gastric mucosal damage.
prevention: Avoiding NSAIDs, alcohol, and smoking; managing stress and treating H. pylori promptly.
treatment: Proton pump inhibitors, antibiotics for H. pylori, and protective agents like sucralfate.

symptoms: Diarrhea, weight loss, bloating, fatigue, and nutrient deficiencies after consuming gluten.
causes: Autoimmune reaction to gluten damaging the small intestine in genetically susceptible individuals.
prevention: Not preventable in predisposed individuals; early diagnosis and strict gluten avoidance are key.
treatment: Lifelong adherence to a strict gluten-free diet and nutritional support.

symptoms: Chronic abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, fatigue, and sometimes rectal bleeding.
causes: Autoimmune inflammation of the digestive tract; exact cause unknown but involves genetics and immune dysregulation.
prevention: No known prevention; managing inflammation and avoiding smoking may help reduce flares.
treatment: Anti-inflammatory drugs, immunosuppressants, biologics (e.g., TNF inhibitors), and sometimes surgery.

symptoms: Abdominal cramps, bloating, gas, diarrhea or constipation, without structural bowel damage.
causes: Unknown; may involve gut-brain interaction, stress, diet, and altered gut motility or microbiota.
prevention: Stress reduction, dietary management, and regular bowel habits.
treatment: Dietary changes (low FODMAP diet), fiber, antispasmodics, probiotics, and behavioral therapy.

symptoms: Painful, swollen veins in the rectum or anus causing bleeding, itching, and discomfort during bowel movements.
causes: Straining during defecation, chronic constipation, pregnancy, or prolonged sitting.
prevention: High-fiber diet, adequate hydration, regular exercise, and avoiding prolonged straining.
treatment: Topical creams, sitz baths, rubber band ligation, or surgical removal in severe cases.

symptoms: Heartburn, acid reflux, chest pain, sour taste in mouth, and regurgitation, especially after meals.
causes: Weakening of the lower esophageal sphincter allowing stomach acid to flow back into the esophagus.
prevention: Avoiding trigger foods, not lying down after eating, weight loss, and elevating the head during sleep.
treatment: Antacids, H2 blockers, proton pump inhibitors, and surgery in refractory cases.

symptoms: Upper abdominal pain, nausea, bloating, indigestion, and sometimes vomiting or loss of appetite.
causes: Irritation of the stomach lining due to H. pylori infection, NSAIDs, alcohol, or stress.
prevention: Avoiding NSAIDs, reducing alcohol, treating infections early, and stress management.
treatment: Proton pump inhibitors, antacids, antibiotics for H. pylori, and dietary modifications.

symptoms: Sudden, intense joint pain (often in the big toe), swelling, redness, and warmth.
causes: Uric acid crystal buildup in joints due to high uric acid levels from diet, genetics, or kidney issues.
prevention: Avoiding purine-rich foods (red meat, shellfish), alcohol, and maintaining hydration.
treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine, corticosteroids for acute attacks, and urate-lowering therapy (e.g., allopurinol).

symptoms: Bone fractures, loss of height, stooped posture, and back pain from vertebral collapse.
causes: Reduced bone density due to aging, hormonal changes (especially postmenopausal), or calcium/vitamin D deficiency.
prevention: Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, weight-bearing exercises, and avoiding smoking or alcohol.
treatment: Bisphosphonates, hormone therapy, calcium and vitamin D supplements, and lifestyle modification.

symptoms: Swollen, stiff, and painful joints, especially in the morning; fatigue and systemic inflammation.
causes: Autoimmune attack on joint lining, influenced by genetic and environmental triggers.
prevention: Not fully preventable, but smoking cessation and early treatment can reduce severity.
treatment: DMARDs (e.g., methotrexate), biologics (e.g., TNF inhibitors), NSAIDs, corticosteroids, and physical therapy.

symptoms: Numbness, weakness, visual disturbances, fatigue, and difficulty with coordination and balance.
causes: Autoimmune attack on the myelin sheath in the central nervous system; exact cause unknown.
prevention: No known prevention; vitamin D and early intervention may help manage risk.
treatment: Immunomodulatory drugs (e.g., interferons, monoclonal antibodies), physical therapy, and symptom management.

symptoms: Hypothyroidism: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance. Hyperthyroidism: weight loss, heat intolerance, tremors, and palpitations.
causes: Autoimmune (Hashimoto’s or Graves’), iodine imbalance, nodules, or thyroiditis.
prevention: Adequate iodine intake, avoiding radiation exposure, and regular screening in high-risk individuals.
treatment: Thyroid hormone replacement (levothyroxine), antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

symptoms: Severe headache, neck stiffness, fever, light sensitivity, confusion, and vomiting.
causes: Infection of meninges by bacteria (e.g., *Neisseria meningitidis*), viruses, or fungi; can also be non-infectious.
prevention: Vaccination (e.g., meningococcal, Hib), avoiding exposure, and prompt treatment of nearby infections.
treatment: Antibiotics for bacterial cases, antiviral or antifungal agents, and supportive care.

symptoms: Loud snoring, daytime fatigue, pauses in breathing during sleep, morning headaches, and poor concentration.
causes: Obstruction of airway during sleep (obstructive), or brain signal issues (central); risk factors include obesity and anatomical abnormalities.
prevention: Weight loss, avoiding alcohol before sleep, and treating nasal congestion.
treatment: CPAP therapy, oral appliances, lifestyle changes, and surgery in some cases.

symptoms: Difficulty falling or staying asleep, early awakening, fatigue, irritability, and poor focus.
causes: Stress, anxiety, depression, poor sleep hygiene, chronic illness, or medication side effects.
prevention: Consistent sleep routine, avoiding caffeine and screens before bed, and managing stress.
treatment: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), sleep hygiene practices, and short-term medications if needed.

symptoms: Mood swings ranging from manic (elevated mood, hyperactivity) to depressive episodes (sadness, low energy).
causes: Genetic, neurochemical, and environmental influences affecting mood regulation.
prevention: Not preventable, but early diagnosis and consistent treatment can reduce impact.
treatment: Mood stabilizers (e.g., lithium), antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and lifestyle regulation.

symptoms: Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, lack of motivation, and cognitive impairment.
causes: Complex interplay of genetic, biochemical, and environmental factors affecting brain function.
prevention: No known prevention; early treatment and avoiding substance abuse may reduce risk.
treatment: Antipsychotic medications, cognitive-behavioral therapy, social support, and rehabilitation programs.

symptoms: Social communication difficulties, restricted interests, repetitive behaviors, and sensory sensitivities.
causes: Neurodevelopmental differences influenced by genetic and environmental factors during early brain development.
prevention: Not preventable; early intervention can improve developmental outcomes.
treatment: Behavioral therapy (ABA), speech and occupational therapy, educational support, and parent training.

symptoms: Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity, difficulty focusing, and trouble organizing tasks or following instructions.
causes: Genetic predisposition, brain structure and function differences, and environmental influences during early development.
prevention: No known prevention; early recognition and consistent support can improve outcomes.
treatment: Behavioral therapy, stimulant medications (e.g., methylphenidate), parent training, and academic accommodations.
